benchmark_details.name: PromptRobust
benchmark_details.overview: PromptRobust is a robustness benchmark designed to measure Large Language Models' (LLMs') resilience to adversarial prompts by applying adversarial textual attacks on prompts across character, word, sentence, and semantic levels and evaluating effects across multiple tasks and datasets.
benchmark_details.data_type: text (adversarial prompts and task samples — e.g., classification examples, question-answering pairs, translation pairs)

purpose_and_intended_users.goal: To evaluate, understand, and analyze the robustness of LLMs to adversarial prompts by generating adversarial prompts (character-, word-, sentence-, and semantic-level) and measuring performance degradation across models, tasks, and datasets.
purpose_and_intended_users.limitations: 1) Evaluations used sampling rather than the full datasets due to computation constraints. 2) Not all LLMs and datasets were included due to computational limits. 3) Did not evaluate more advanced prompt-engineering techniques (e.g., chain-of-thought, tree-of-thought). 4) Considered black-box prompt attacks; white-box optimized prompt attacks were not evaluated.

data.source: Public datasets across GLUE (SST-2, CoLA, QQP, MRPC, MNLI, QNLI, RTE, WNLI), MMLU, SQuAD V2, UN Multi, IWSLT 2017, and Mathematics (DeepMind Mathematics Dataset). Adversarial prompts are generated by adapting attacks (TextBugger, DeepWordBug, TextFooler, BertAttack, CheckList, StressTest, semantic translations).
data.size: 4,788 adversarial prompts generated; evaluated across 13 datasets. Per-dataset sample counts (as reported): SST-2: 872 samples; CoLA: 1,000; QQP: 1,000; MRPC: 408; MNLI: 1,000; QNLI: 1,000; RTE: 277; WNLI: 71; MMLU: 564; SQuAD V2: 200; UN Multi: 99; IWSLT 2017: 100; Math: 160.
data.annotation: Adversarial prompts generated algorithmically by adapted textual attacks (character-, word-, sentence-, semantic-level). Human evaluation: five volunteers assessed semantic preservation of sampled adversarial prompts (at least 85% acceptable). Original datasets retain their provided ground-truth labels.

methods: Automated evaluation using task-specific metrics (classification accuracy, F1-score for reading comprehension, BLEU for translation)
Unified metric: Performance Drop Rate (PDR) and Average PDR (APDR)
Attention visualization (Attention by Gradient and Attention by Deletion)
Transferability analysis of adversarial prompts between models
Human evaluation for semantic preservation of adversarial prompts
methodology.calculation: PDR(A,P,fθ,D) = 1 − (∑_{(x,y)∈D} M[fθ([A(P),x]),y]) / (∑_{(x,y)∈D} M[fθ([P,x]),y]), where A is the adversarial attack applied to prompt P, fθ is the model, D is the dataset, and M[·] is the evaluation function (indicator for classification, F1 for reading comprehension, BLEU for translation). APDR is the average of PDR over prompts/models/attacks as defined in the paper.
methodology.interpretation: PDR quantifies the relative performance decline after a prompt attack. Higher PDR indicates larger degradation in performance; a negative PDR implies adversarial prompts can sometimes improve performance.
methodology.baseline_results: Baseline (clean-prompt) model results are provided in Table 9. Examples include T5-large SST-2: 94.79 ±0.49 (accuracy), UL2 SST-2: 95.92 ±1.03 (accuracy), GPT-4 MMLU: 63.33 ±2.56. Detailed baselines for all evaluated models and datasets are reported in Table 9 of the paper.
methodology.validation: Human study: five volunteers judged semantic preservation of 100 sampled adversarial prompts (≥85% acceptable). Sampling strategy for datasets (e.g., sample sizes for GLUE, MMLU, SQuAD V2, translation, Math) described in Appendix C. Evaluation performed across multiple LLMs, attacks, prompt types, and datasets; attention visualization and transferability analyses used to validate observed effects.
